sourceTitle: Start fresh and do the thing: How to actually set and hit team goals this year

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of linking the Roadmap and Projects databases in Notion?

options.text: It automatically syncs all team members' calendars.
options.explanation: Linking databases does not affect calendar sync; it only connects related records.

options.text: It lets you see projects associated with each initiative in one place.
options.explanation: Correct—relation properties show which projects support each initiative, giving a full line of sight.

options.text: It creates a backup of all project files in the Roadmap.
options.explanation: The link does not duplicate files; it merely references related items.

options.text: It enforces a strict workflow that cannot be changed.
options.explanation: Notion remains flexible; linking databases does not lock down workflows.

questions.question: In the hierarchy described in the guide, which element comes directly after “initiatives”?

options.text: Company goals
options.explanation: Company goals are higher‑level; they precede initiatives.

options.text: Team goals
options.explanation: Team goals sit above initiatives, not below.

options.text: Projects
options.explanation: The guide maps the flow as projects → initiatives → team goals → company goals, so projects follow initiatives.

options.text: Tasks
options.explanation: Tasks are not part of the described hierarchy.

questions.question: Which property should you add to the Roadmap database to clearly identify who is responsible for each initiative?

options.text: Date property
options.explanation: Date tracks timelines, not ownership.

options.text: Person property
options.explanation: A Person property lets you assign an owner (or DRI) to each initiative.

options.text: Multi‑select property for quarters
options.explanation: Multi‑select for quarters indicates timing, not ownership.

options.text: Number property for budget
options.explanation: Budget numbers are unrelated to assigning owners.

questions.question: When creating a relation between the Roadmap and Projects tables, where do you configure this property?

options.text: In the Projects table, add a Formula property that references the Roadmap.
options.explanation: Relations are not set up via formulas; they use a dedicated Relation property.

options.text: In the Roadmap table, add a Relation property that points to the Projects database.
options.explanation: The guide instructs you to create a Relation property in Roadmap linking to Projects.

options.text: Create a linked database view of Projects inside the Roadmap page.
options.explanation: Linked views display data but don’t create a relational link between records.

options.text: Add a Select property in Projects with the names of initiatives.
options.explanation: Select properties are static lists; they don’t create a true relational connection.

questions.question: Which custom view would best help a manager see how many projects each team member is handling?

options.text: A Calendar view filtered by launch dates.
options.explanation: Calendar shows dates, not distribution of work per person.

options.text: A Board view grouped by the DRI (person) property.
options.explanation: Grouping the Board by DRI displays each person’s projects side‑by‑side.

options.text: A Table view sorted by priority.
options.explanation: Sorting by priority doesn’t reveal workload per individual.

options.text: A Timeline view organized by end date.
options.explanation: Timeline visualizes schedules, not who owns each project.

questions.question: What is the purpose of using a Multi‑select property for “Q1, Q2, etc.” in the Roadmap database?

options.text: To assign each initiative a budget amount per quarter.
options.explanation: Budget amounts would use a Number property, not Multi‑select.

options.text: To indicate which quarters an initiative spans.
options.explanation: Multi‑select lets you tag an initiative with one or more quarters, showing its timeline.

options.text: To rank initiatives by importance within each quarter.
options.explanation: Ranking would be done with a Select or Number property, not Multi‑select.

options.text: To link initiatives to external calendar events.
options.explanation: Linking to external calendars uses Date or URL properties, not Multi‑select.

questions.question: When setting up a Timeline view for the Projects database, which property should you select under “Timeline by”?

options.text: The “Status” Select property.
options.explanation: Status is categorical, not a date, so it can’t drive a timeline.

options.text: The “Launch date” Date property.
options.explanation: Launch date alone shows a single point, not a range; the guide suggests using the execution timeline property.

options.text: The “Execution timeline” Date property with both start and end dates.
options.explanation: Choosing the execution timeline (a date range) lets the Timeline view display the full span of each project.

options.text: The “Type” Select property.
options.explanation: Type categorizes projects but does not contain date information.

questions.question: Why does the guide recommend using database templates in the Projects database?

options.text: To automatically assign owners to new projects.
options.explanation: Templates provide structure, but ownership still needs to be set per project.

options.text: To ensure each project follows a consistent set of criteria and process.
options.explanation: Templates standardize the information and steps required for each type of project.

options.text: To lock the project pages so they can’t be edited.
options.explanation: Templates are editable; they don’t make pages read‑only.

options.text: To hide completed projects from the main view.
options.explanation: Visibility is controlled by filters, not by using templates.

questions.question: How does linking a filtered view of the Projects database into a weekly 1:1 template benefit the meeting?

options.text: It automatically marks all listed projects as completed after the meeting.
options.explanation: Linking a view does not change the status of items; it only displays them.

options.text: It provides a real‑time snapshot of the direct report’s current projects, streamlining status updates.
options.explanation: A linked, filtered view shows only that person’s projects, keeping the 1:1 focused and up‑to‑date.

options.text: It creates a backup of the Projects database each week.
options.explanation: Linking does not duplicate or back up data.

options.text: It disables editing of the Projects database during the meeting.
options.explanation: Views remain editable unless permissions are changed; linking does not lock editing.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT listed as a recommended custom view for the Roadmap database?

options.text: A filtered view for each person on the team.
options.explanation: The guide explicitly mentions this view.

options.text: A Board view grouped by status.
options.explanation: A Board grouped by status is recommended.

options.text: A Timeline view showing initiative start dates.
options.explanation: The guide does not list a Timeline view for Roadmap; it suggests Table views per quarter.

options.text: Table views for each quarter (Q1‑Q4).
options.explanation: Quarterly Table views are part of the recommendation.
